# Broth microdilution

Broth microdilution is a standardized reference method in [microbiology](/page/Microbiology) for determining the [minimum inhibitory concentration](/page/Minimum_inhibitory_concentration) (MIC) of [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) agents against [bacteria](/page/Bacteria), involving serial twofold dilutions of antibiotics in small volumes of [broth](/page/Broth) within 96-well microtiter plates.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1) This technique allows for the precise quantification of the lowest concentration that inhibits visible [bacterial growth](/page/Bacterial_growth) after incubation, typically at 35–37°C for 16–20 hours.[[[2]](#ref-2)](#ref-2) It is widely used in both clinical diagnostics and research to guide antibiotic therapy, monitor resistance patterns, and evaluate new [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) compounds.[[[3]](#ref-3)](#ref-3) Broth dilution methods originated with Alexander Fleming's 1929 serial dilution tests for penicillin susceptibility. Broth microdilution was pioneered in 1977 to enable high-throughput testing using small volumes in microtiter plates.[[[4]](#ref-4)](#ref-4) Standardized by organizations such as the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) and the European Committee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing (EUCAST), broth microdilution is recommended as the gold standard for certain antibiotics like [colistin](/page/Colistin) due to its high precision and ability to test multiple agents simultaneously in a single plate.[[[5]](#ref-5)](#ref-5) Its advantages include low reagent volumes, cost-effectiveness for routine use, and adaptability for fastidious organisms or [multidrug-resistant bacteria](/page/Multidrug-resistant_bacteria) through modifications in media or incubation conditions.[[[3]](#ref-3)](#ref-3) Compared to disk diffusion or agar dilution, it provides quantitative MIC data essential for pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic modeling and susceptibility breakpoints.[[[6]](#ref-6)](#ref-6)

## Introduction

### Definition and Purpose

Broth microdilution is a standardized quantitative technique employed in [microbiology](/page/Microbiology) to evaluate [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) susceptibility by preparing serial dilutions of antibiotics or antifungals in [liquid](/page/Liquid) [broth](/page/Broth) medium within the wells of a microtiter plate, typically a 96-well format, followed by [inoculation](/page/Inoculation) with a standardized microbial suspension.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1)[[[7]](#ref-7)](#ref-7) This method allows for the precise determination of the [minimum inhibitory concentration](/page/Minimum_inhibitory_concentration) (MIC), defined as the lowest concentration of the [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) agent that inhibits visible growth of the [microorganism](/page/Microorganism) after a specified [incubation period](/page/Incubation_period), usually 16–24 hours at 35–37°C.[[[8]](#ref-8)](#ref-8)[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1) The MIC endpoint is typically assessed by [visual inspection](/page/Visual_inspection) for [turbidity](/page/Turbidity) or colorimetric indicators, providing a measurable threshold for microbial inhibition.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1)[[[7]](#ref-7)](#ref-7) The primary purpose of broth microdilution is to assess the [in vitro](/page/In_vitro) susceptibility of bacterial or fungal isolates to specific [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) agents, enabling clinicians to select appropriate therapies tailored to the pathogen's sensitivity profile.[[[9]](#ref-9)](#ref-9)[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1) By generating MIC values, the technique facilitates the categorization of isolates as susceptible, intermediate, or resistant according to established clinical breakpoints, thereby guiding treatment decisions to optimize [patient](/page/Patient) outcomes and reduce the risk of therapeutic failure.[[[7]](#ref-7)](#ref-7)[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1) Furthermore, it plays a vital role in [antimicrobial resistance](/page/Antimicrobial_resistance) surveillance programs, where aggregated MIC data from diverse isolates help track resistance trends, inform [public health](/page/Public_health) policies, and support the development of new therapeutic strategies.[[[9]](#ref-9)](#ref-9)[[[8]](#ref-8)](#ref-8) In the context of [pharmacodynamics](/page/Pharmacodynamics), the MIC serves as a foundational metric that correlates [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) exposure with microbial killing, linking [in vitro](/page/In_vitro) results to [in vivo](/page/In_vivo) [efficacy](/page/Efficacy) and aiding in the [determination](/page/Determination) of optimal dosing regimens to achieve concentrations above the MIC threshold for effective treatment.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1)[[[7]](#ref-7)](#ref-7) This method is recognized as the gold standard for MIC [determination](/page/Determination) and is standardized by authoritative bodies such as the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) to ensure reproducibility and reliability across laboratories.[[[9]](#ref-9)](#ref-9)[[[8]](#ref-8)](#ref-8)

### Historical Development

Broth microdilution emerged in the [1960s](/page/1960s) and [1970s](/page/1970s) as a refinement of earlier macro-dilution techniques, which originated in the late 1920s with Alexander Fleming's [serial dilution](/page/Serial_dilution) method for assessing [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) activity through dilutions in larger volumes of liquid media.[[[10]](#ref-10)](#ref-10) This miniaturization leveraged 96-well microtiter plates, initially developed by Hungarian virologist Gyula Takátsy in 1951–1955 to combat an [influenza](/page/Influenza) outbreak by enabling high-throughput serological testing, and later adapted for bacterial [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) susceptibility testing in clinical laboratories to enhance efficiency and reduce reagent use.[[[11]](#ref-11)](#ref-11) By the mid-[1970s](/page/1970s), the method gained traction for its ability to perform multiple tests simultaneously with microliter-scale volumes (approximately 100 μL), marking a significant improvement over labor-intensive macro methods.[[[4]](#ref-4)](#ref-4) Key innovations in the [1970s](/page/1970s) included [automation](/page/Automation) of plate preparation and reading, exemplified by Dynatech Laboratories' introduction of the MIC-2000 system around 1978, a dispensing instrument that standardized [antibiotic](/page/Antibiotic) dilutions in microtiter trays and improved reproducibility for routine susceptibility testing.[[[12]](#ref-12)](#ref-12) Standardization efforts accelerated in the 1980s through the National Committee for Clinical Laboratory Standards (NCCLS, now the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute or CLSI), which published the first tentative guideline (M7-T) in 1979 for broth dilution methods, followed by approved standards that formalized broth microdilution protocols for determining minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of aerobic [bacteria](/page/Bacteria). These NCCLS/CLSI documents addressed variability in media, inoculum, and incubation conditions, establishing the method as a reference standard for clinical [microbiology](/page/Microbiology). Parallel standardization efforts by the European Committee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing (EUCAST), established in 1997, have further harmonized broth microdilution methods internationally.[[[13]](#ref-13)](#ref-13)[[[14]](#ref-14)](#ref-14) The 1990s and 2000s saw a shift from manual to fully automated commercial systems, incorporating broth microdilution principles for high-throughput analysis. The VITEK system, originating from NASA-sponsored [research](/page/Research) in the 1960s and commercialized by [bioMérieux](/page/BioMérieux) in the 1980s (with VITEK 2 launched around 2000), automated identification and susceptibility testing using miniaturized cards based on microdilution technology.[[[15]](#ref-15)](#ref-15) Similarly, Becton Dickinson's Phoenix system, cleared by the FDA in 2003, integrated optical detection for rapid MIC results in automated panels.[[[16]](#ref-16)](#ref-16) CLSI guidelines evolved to tackle emerging resistance, with the 12th edition of M07 in 2024 updating broth microdilution procedures for specific antimicrobials and media supplements to ensure relevance against multidrug-resistant pathogens.[[[17]](#ref-17)](#ref-17)

## Principles and Methodology

### Underlying Principles

Broth microdilution is grounded in the principle of preparing serial two-fold dilutions of [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) agents in a nutrient broth medium within the wells of a microtiter plate, establishing a concentration gradient that spans from high to low levels of the agent. This setup enables the identification of the threshold concentration at which bacterial growth is inhibited, known as the [minimum inhibitory concentration](/page/Minimum_inhibitory_concentration) (MIC). The dilution process follows the equation $ C\_n = \frac{C\_0}{2^n} $, where $ C\_n $ is the concentration in the nth well, $ C\_0 $ is the initial concentration, and $ n $ represents the dilution step, typically resulting in twofold reductions across 8-12 wells per row.[[[18]](#ref-18)](#ref-18) At its microbiological foundation, the method exploits the logarithmic (exponential) growth phase of [bacteria](/page/Bacteria) in liquid [broth](/page/Broth), where cells replicate rapidly and are most susceptible to [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial)s. Antimicrobials exert their effects through specific mechanisms, such as disrupting cell wall synthesis (e.g., beta-lactams targeting [peptidoglycan](/page/Peptidoglycan) assembly) or blocking protein synthesis (e.g., aminoglycosides interfering with ribosomal function), leading to halted replication or [cell death](/page/Cell_death). The MIC is defined as the lowest concentration preventing visible bacterial growth, manifested as the absence of [turbidity](/page/Turbidity) in the well, which indicates that the antimicrobial has effectively suppressed proliferation to below detectable levels under standard conditions.[[[19]](#ref-19)](#ref-19)[[[18]](#ref-18)](#ref-18) Critical factors influencing the reliability of this method include the [standardization](/page/Standardization) of the bacterial inoculum at approximately $ 5 \times 10^5 $ colony-forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL) in the final well volume, ensuring consistent starting densities for comparable growth inhibition assessments. Incubation occurs under aerobic conditions at 35-37°C for 16-20 hours, promoting uniform [logarithmic growth](/page/Logarithmic_growth) while mimicking physiological temperatures relevant to clinical infections. Endpoint determination relies on [visual inspection](/page/Visual_inspection) for lack of [turbidity](/page/Turbidity) or, alternatively, spectrophotometric measurement of optical density to quantify growth absence objectively.[[[18]](#ref-18)](#ref-18)

### Materials and Equipment

The broth microdilution method relies on standardized materials and equipment to facilitate precise serial dilutions of [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial)s and consistent assessment of [bacterial growth](/page/Bacterial_growth) inhibition, as outlined in established guidelines for antimicrobial susceptibility testing. Essential consumables include sterile, disposable [pipette](/page/Pipette) tips compatible with multichannel pipettes to prevent cross-contamination during dispensing, along with 0.85% saline solution for preparing bacterial suspensions and dilutions. Positive growth controls consist of bacterial inoculum in [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial)-free [broth](/page/Broth) to verify [organism](/page/Organism) viability, while negative controls use uninoculated [broth](/page/Broth) to confirm sterility.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1) Microtiter plates are 96-well [polystyrene](/page/Polystyrene) trays, typically featuring U- or V-bottom wells to promote bacterial pelleting and improve visibility during [turbidity](/page/Turbidity) evaluation, with each well holding approximately 200 μL of [volume](/page/Volume).[[[20]](#ref-20)](#ref-20) Key [reagents](/page/List_of_reagents) encompass cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton [broth](/page/Broth) (CAMHB) as the base medium, formulated to maintain consistent divalent cation levels (50-90 mg/L calcium and 20-40 mg/L magnesium) for reproducible [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) activity across bacterial [species](/page/Species). Antimicrobial stock solutions are prepared from [reference](/page/Reference) powders of known potency, typically at concentrations of 1000-5120 μg/mL in appropriate solvents, and stored frozen until use. The bacterial inoculum is standardized by suspending fresh colonies in saline to a 0.5 McFarland [turbidity](/page/Turbidity) standard (equivalent to 1-2 × 10^8 CFU/mL), then further diluted in CAMHB to a working concentration of approximately 5 × 10^5 CFU/mL per well.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1)[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1) Required equipment includes multichannel [pipettes](/page/The_Pipettes) (8- or 12-channel models adjustable for 50-200 μL volumes) for simultaneous dispensing into multiple wells, enhancing efficiency and minimizing variability. An incubator maintained at 35°C ± 2°C provides the controlled aerobic environment for overnight incubation, usually 16-20 hours. Results are assessed visually using a plate-reading mirror to detect [turbidity](/page/Turbidity) or quantitatively via a spectrophotometer or [plate reader](/page/Plate_reader) measuring optical density at 600 nm.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1)

### Step-by-Step Procedure

The broth microdilution procedure involves preparing serial dilutions of [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) agents in a 96-well microtiter plate, followed by [inoculation](/page/Inoculation) with a standardized bacterial suspension, inclusion of appropriate controls, and incubation to assess growth inhibition.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1) **Preparation of antimicrobial dilutions:** Begin by selecting a suitable [broth](/page/Broth) medium, such as cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton [broth](/page/Broth), to maintain physiological conditions for bacterial growth. Prepare [stock](/page/Stock) solutions of the [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) agent at a concentration twice the highest desired final concentration (e.g., 256 μg/mL for a starting final concentration of 128 μg/mL), using appropriate solvents and diluents as specified for stability. Prepare serial two-fold dilutions of the [stock](/page/Stock) in the [broth](/page/Broth) medium at twice the desired final concentrations (e.g., 256, 128, 64, ..., 0.5 μg/mL) in separate tubes or a master dilution plate. Dispense 50 μL of each dilution into the corresponding wells (columns 1 through 10 or 11) of a sterile 96-well flat-bottom microtiter plate. Add 50 μL of [broth](/page/Broth) alone to control wells (column 11 or 12). This setup ensures a logarithmic concentration gradient for precise [minimum inhibitory concentration](/page/Minimum_inhibitory_concentration) (MIC) determination, based on the principles of [serial dilution](/page/Serial_dilution).[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1)[[[21]](#ref-21)](#ref-21)[[[22]](#ref-22)](#ref-22)[[[23]](#ref-23)](#ref-23) **Inoculation:** Standardize the bacterial inoculum by adjusting an overnight [culture](/page/Culture) to a 0.5 McFarland [turbidity](/page/Turbidity) standard, equivalent to approximately 1.5 × 10^8 CFU/mL, using a spectrophotometer or [turbidity](/page/Turbidity) meter. Dilute this suspension 1:100 in the same [broth](/page/Broth) medium (yielding approximately 1.5 × 10^6 CFU/mL) to achieve the working inoculum. Add 50 μL of the diluted inoculum to each well containing the [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) dilutions (columns 1–10 or 11) and the growth control wells, resulting in a final volume of 100 μL per well and a final inoculum density of approximately 5 × 10^5 CFU/mL. Perform this step promptly to avoid changes in viability, and use a multichannel [pipette](/page/Pipette) for efficiency and consistency across replicates.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1)[[[22]](#ref-22)](#ref-22)[[[21]](#ref-21)](#ref-21)[[[23]](#ref-23)](#ref-23) **Controls:** Include a growth control by adding 50 μL of the inoculum to 50 μL of [broth](/page/Broth)-only wells (typically column 11 or 12) to confirm bacterial viability and expected growth. Set up a sterility control with 100 μL of [broth](/page/Broth) alone (no inoculum) in the remaining wells of column 12 to detect [contamination](/page/Contamination). Incorporate quality control strains, such as *[Escherichia coli](/page/Escherichia_coli)* ATCC 25922, in separate panels on the same plate or a dedicated plate to validate the test's performance against established MIC ranges. These controls ensure the reliability of the assay and adherence to standardized protocols.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1)[[[22]](#ref-22)](#ref-22) **Incubation and reading:** Seal the plate with a breathable adhesive film to prevent [evaporation](/page/Evaporation) and [contamination](/page/Contamination), then incubate at 35–37°C in ambient air for 16–20 hours, using a stationary or shaking incubator as appropriate for the [organism](/page/Organism). After incubation, examine the wells visually under good [lighting](/page/Lighting) against a white background for [turbidity](/page/Turbidity); clear wells indicate inhibition. For quantitative assessment, use a [microplate](/page/Microplate) reader to measure optical density at 600 nm ([OD600](/page/OD600)), considering wells with OD600 < 0.1 as no growth relative to the growth control. Avoid disturbing the plate before reading to prevent false results from settling.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1)[[[22]](#ref-22)](#ref-22)[[[21]](#ref-21)](#ref-21)

## Interpretation and Analysis

### Determining Minimum Inhibitory Concentration

The minimum inhibitory concentration ([MIC](/page/Minimum_inhibitory_concentration)) in broth microdilution is defined as the lowest concentration of an antimicrobial agent that completely inhibits visible growth of the inoculum organism, corresponding to 100% inhibition relative to the growth control well.[[[24]](#ref-24)](#ref-24) This endpoint is determined after incubation by [visual inspection](/page/Visual_inspection) on a non-reflecting surface, using an unaided eye or reading mirror to identify the first clear well lacking [turbidity](/page/Turbidity), button formation, or thin film.[[[24]](#ref-24)](#ref-24) For ambiguous cases, such as partial growth or reduced but visible turbidity, guidelines specify alternative thresholds: the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) and European Committee on [Antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) Susceptibility Testing (EUCAST) recommend reading the MIC at 80% growth reduction for agents like trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, while 50% inhibition may apply in specific contexts for other drugs, though 100% remains the default for most [antimicrobials](/page/Antimicrobial).[[[24]](#ref-24)](#ref-24)[[[25]](#ref-25)](#ref-25) MIC values are reported in μg/mL (or mg/L), using a dilution series format such as ≤0.5, 1, 2, 4, up to the highest tested concentration, ensuring the value reflects the precise twofold dilution step where inhibition occurs.[[[24]](#ref-24)](#ref-24) These MICs are then categorized as susceptible (S), intermediate (I), or resistant (R) based on organism-specific breakpoints from CLSI M100 or EUCAST tables, which integrate pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic data to guide clinical decisions. Common issues include trailing endpoints, characterized by partial growth (e.g., faint haze or small buttons) in wells above the true MIC, particularly with bacteriostatic agents like tetracyclines or sulfonamides; these are resolved by reading at complete (100%) inhibition and ignoring minor pinpoint growth unless specified otherwise.[[[24]](#ref-24)](#ref-24)[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1) As an optional follow-up to assess bactericidal activity, the [minimum bactericidal concentration](/page/Minimum_bactericidal_concentration) (MBC) can be determined by subculturing 10 μL from wells at or above the MIC onto [agar](/page/Agar) plates without [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial); the MBC is the lowest concentration yielding fewer than 5 colonies (≥99.9% kill) after overnight incubation.[[[26]](#ref-26)](#ref-26) For example, in a twofold dilution series of an antimicrobial (e.g., 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8 μg/mL) showing visible growth (turbidity) up to 4 μg/mL and complete inhibition starting at 8 μg/mL, the MIC is reported as 8 μg/mL.[[[24]](#ref-24)](#ref-24)

### Quality Control Measures

Quality control measures are essential to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of broth microdilution results, adhering to standardized protocols outlined in CLSI guidelines.[[[27]](#ref-27)](#ref-27) These measures involve routine testing of reference strains to validate the performance of the assay, including inoculum preparation, media quality, and antimicrobial agent activity. Control strains recommended by CLSI, such as *Staphylococcus aureus* ATCC 29213 and *Enterococcus faecalis* ATCC 29212, are used for quality control and should be tested daily or weekly depending on laboratory volume. For example, the expected minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) range for oxacillin against *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 is 0.12–0.5 μg/mL in cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth.[[[28]](#ref-28)](#ref-28) These strains are selected for their stable susceptibility profiles and are commercially available from repositories like ATCC to minimize variability.[[[29]](#ref-29)](#ref-29) Validation steps include verifying inoculum accuracy using a spectrophotometer to achieve a 0.5 McFarland standard (approximately 1–2 × 10^8 CFU/mL prior to dilution), ensuring consistent bacterial density across tests.[[[27]](#ref-27)](#ref-27) Media sterility is confirmed by incubating uninoculated control wells, which should show no growth after the standard incubation period, while antimicrobial potency for in-house preparations can be assessed via [high-performance liquid chromatography](/page/High-performance_liquid_chromatography) (HPLC) if degradation is suspected.[[[27]](#ref-27)](#ref-27) Troubleshooting addresses potential sources of error, such as false positives due to [contamination](/page/Contamination), which can be resolved by repeating the test with fresh media and verifying sterility; false negatives may result from under-incubation or incorrect [temperature](/page/Temperature), necessitating checks of equipment calibration.[[[1]](#ref-1)](#ref-1) For fastidious organisms, environmental factors like CO2 levels (5% for certain species) must be monitored to prevent growth inhibition.[[[27]](#ref-27)](#ref-27) Documentation is critical for compliance and continuous improvement, involving logging of all QC results with dates, observed MICs, and any deviations, alongside participation in external proficiency testing programs such as those offered by the [College of American Pathologists](/page/College_of_American_Pathologists) ([CAP](/page/Cap)) to benchmark laboratory performance against peers. If QC results fall outside established ranges, testing is halted until the issue is resolved and documented.

## Advantages and Limitations

### Advantages

Broth microdilution offers quantitative precision by determining the exact [minimum inhibitory concentration](/page/Minimum_inhibitory_concentration) (MIC) of antimicrobials, enabling precise dose optimization in clinical settings.[[[30]](#ref-30)](#ref-30) This method provides numerical MIC values, contrasting with qualitative approaches that only indicate susceptibility categories without specific concentration [data](/page/Data).[[[31]](#ref-31)](#ref-31) The resulting MICs support pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic modeling for tailored therapy, improving treatment efficacy against resistant pathogens.[[[32]](#ref-32)](#ref-32) The 96-well microtiter plate format facilitates high throughput, allowing simultaneous testing of multiple antimicrobials against various isolates in a single run.[[[19]](#ref-19)](#ref-19) Each well typically requires only 50-100 μL of [broth](/page/Broth), significantly reducing [reagent](/page/Reagent) volumes compared to traditional tube-based macrodilution methods that use 1-2 mL per test.[[[33]](#ref-33)](#ref-33) Recent optimizations, such as using reduced volumes in 384-well plates, further lower [reagent](/page/Reagent) consumption and environmental impact while maintaining MIC accuracy.[[[33]](#ref-33)](#ref-33) This efficiency minimizes hands-on time and supports high throughput when integrated with [automation](/page/Automation).[[[34]](#ref-34)](#ref-34) Standardization through Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) protocols ensures high reproducibility across laboratories, with inter- and intralaboratory agreement often exceeding 90% for MIC results within one to two dilutions.[[[35]](#ref-35)](#ref-35) These guidelines define precise conditions for inoculum preparation, incubation, and endpoint reading, promoting consistent outcomes in diverse settings.[[[36]](#ref-36)](#ref-36) Cost-effectiveness arises from reduced media and reagent consumption, making the method scalable for large-scale antimicrobial surveillance programs, such as those monitoring [methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus](/page/Methicillin-resistant_Staphylococcus_aureus) (MRSA) prevalence.[[[37]](#ref-37)](#ref-37) The low per-test volume lowers overall expenses, enabling broader implementation in resource-limited environments without compromising accuracy.[[[38]](#ref-38)](#ref-38)

### Limitations

Broth microdilution susceptibility testing demands high technical precision in pipetting [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) agents and preparing standardized bacterial inocula, typically adjusted to a 0.5 McFarland standard to achieve 5 × 10^5 CFU/mL in the final well. Errors in these manual steps, such as inconsistent volumes or inoculum density, can propagate through serial dilutions, resulting in MIC variability greater than one twofold dilution step in up to 5-10% of tests without proper controls.[[[39]](#ref-39)](#ref-39)[[[40]](#ref-40)](#ref-40) The procedure requires 18-24 hours of incubation for most aerobic [bacteria](/page/Bacteria), extending to 48 hours or more for certain organisms, which delays clinical decision-making compared to faster phenotypic methods. While manual setups use inexpensive materials, automating the process with systems like the BD Phoenix or VITEK involves substantial upfront investment for equipment and software integration.[[[41]](#ref-41)](#ref-41)[[[42]](#ref-42)](#ref-42) Scope limitations restrict its applicability to fastidious anaerobes, such as [Clostridium](/page/Clostridium) species, or slow-growing pathogens like [Mycobacterium tuberculosis](/page/Mycobacterium_tuberculosis), where poor growth in standard cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth necessitates specialized anaerobic chambers, supplemented media, or alternative protocols. Similarly, the method struggles to assess resistance in [biofilm](/page/Biofilm)-forming bacteria, as planktonic cell testing overlooks the protective matrix that can increase tolerance by 10-1000 fold, requiring biofilm-specific adaptations like pre-formed biofilm models.[[[43]](#ref-43)](#ref-43)[[[44]](#ref-44)](#ref-44) Interpretive challenges arise because the MIC reflects static in vitro inhibition under controlled conditions and does not reliably predict in vivo efficacy, influenced by host factors like [immune response](/page/Immune_response) or tissue penetration. Results are also sensitive to environmental variables, including media pH (ideally 7.2-7.4) and cation levels (e.g., magnesium and calcium), which can alter [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) activity by up to twofold. [Quality control](/page/Quality_control) measures, such as testing reference strains daily, can help reduce technical variability but do not fully address these inherent interpretive constraints.[[[45]](#ref-45)](#ref-45)[[[41]](#ref-41)](#ref-41)

## Comparisons with Other Methods

### Versus Disk Diffusion

Broth microdilution employs serial two-fold dilutions of antibiotics in liquid media within 96-well microtiter plates, enabling quantitative determination of the [minimum inhibitory concentration](/page/Minimum_inhibitory_concentration) (MIC) by assessing bacterial growth inhibition after overnight incubation. In contrast, disk diffusion involves spreading a standardized bacterial inoculum on Mueller-Hinton [agar](/page/Agar) plates, applying antibiotic-impregnated disks, and measuring the diameter of clear zones of inhibition following 16–18 hours of incubation to categorize susceptibility qualitatively.[[[46]](#ref-46)](#ref-46) The primary outcome of broth microdilution is a precise numerical MIC value, which facilitates pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) correlations for optimizing dosing regimens, whereas disk diffusion provides categorical results—susceptible (S), intermediate (I), or resistant (R)—based on zone diameters, such as ≥21 mm indicating susceptibility for ceftazidime against [Enterobacterales](/page/Enterobacterales) (30 μg disk).[[[46]](#ref-46)](#ref-46)[[[28]](#ref-28)](#ref-28) While broth microdilution offers superior resolution for detecting subtle resistance shifts, it is more labor-intensive and expensive (costing $10–$22 per panel) compared to the simpler, lower-cost ($2.50–$5) disk diffusion method.[[[46]](#ref-46)](#ref-46) CLSI breakpoints harmonize interpretations between the two methods by linking MIC thresholds to corresponding zone sizes, yet discrepancies occur in 10–20% of tests, often with beta-lactams where MICs near breakpoints (e.g., 4–16 mg/L for ceftazidime-avibactam) yield inconsistent S/I/R categorizations.[[[47]](#ref-47)](#ref-47) Disk diffusion incubation is typically faster at 18 hours and suitable for broad screening, but it lacks the precision of broth microdilution for values close to clinical breakpoints, potentially leading to very major errors (false susceptible) in up to 7.5% of cases.[[[47]](#ref-47)](#ref-47)[[[46]](#ref-46)](#ref-46) In practice, broth microdilution is the reference method for confirmatory testing and research requiring exact MICs, such as in antimicrobial stewardship programs, while disk diffusion remains the workhorse for routine clinical screening due to its accessibility and flexibility in resource-limited settings.[[[46]](#ref-46)](#ref-46)

### Versus Gradient Diffusion (E-test)

Broth microdilution and the E-test (also known as [gradient](/page/Gradient) diffusion) are both quantitative methods for determining the [minimum inhibitory concentration](/page/Minimum_inhibitory_concentration) (MIC) of antimicrobials against [bacteria](/page/Bacteria), but they differ fundamentally in their approaches. In broth microdilution, antimicrobials are serially diluted across wells in a microtiter plate containing a standardized inoculum in liquid medium, allowing for the assessment of a full range of concentrations in a 96-well format. In contrast, the E-test employs a [plastic](/page/Plastic) strip impregnated with a predefined exponential [gradient](/page/Gradient) of the antimicrobial (typically spanning 0.016–256 µg/mL), which is placed on an [agar plate](/page/Agar_plate) inoculated with the test organism; the MIC is read at the point where the edge of the inhibition ellipse intersects the strip. This [gradient](/page/Gradient) method eliminates the need for manual dilutions, simplifying preparation for individual agents.[[[46]](#ref-46)](#ref-46) Both techniques yield precise MIC values, facilitating breakpoint interpretation according to standards like CLSI or EUCAST, but they offer distinct practical advantages depending on the context. The E-test is particularly convenient for testing single [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) agents, as it requires no pre-prepared dilutions and can be applied directly to [agar](/page/Agar) media tailored for fastidious organisms, such as those requiring [chocolate agar](/page/Chocolate_agar) or CO2 supplementation.[[[19]](#ref-19)](#ref-19) Broth microdilution, however, provides greater standardization for susceptibility panels, enabling simultaneous testing of multiple drugs in a single plate, which is ideal for routine high-throughput workflows. For fastidious species like [Haemophilus influenzae](/page/Haemophilus_influenzae) or anaerobes, the E-test's [agar](/page/Agar)-based format often performs better due to easier adaptation of growth conditions compared to the liquid environment of broth microdilution.[[[48]](#ref-48)](#ref-48) Studies evaluating the correlation between the two methods consistently report high essential agreement, typically exceeding 90% within ±1 log2 dilution of the MIC, indicating reliable interchangeability for most clinical isolates.[[[49]](#ref-49)](#ref-49) For instance, in testing [colistin](/page/Colistin) against [Enterobacteriaceae](/page/Enterobacteriaceae), agreement rates reached 97% essential and 95% categorical concordance.[[[50]](#ref-50)](#ref-50) However, discrepancies can occur, with the E-test tending to overestimate MICs—particularly on Mueller-Hinton agar—due to [diffusion](/page/Diffusion) dynamics and edge-reading variability, potentially leading to minor errors in susceptibility categorization for border-line isolates.[[[51]](#ref-51)](#ref-51) [Cost](/page/Cost) considerations also favor broth microdilution for broader applications; E-test strips range from $2–5 per test, while commercial broth panels cost approximately $1 per equivalent determination when amortized across multiple agents.[[[46]](#ref-46)](#ref-46) In practice, the choice between methods aligns with laboratory needs: broth microdilution suits high-volume routine testing of standardized panels, whereas the E-test excels for confirmatory testing of non-standard or fastidious organisms, or when rapid setup for a single drug is prioritized.[[[19]](#ref-19)](#ref-19) This complementary use enhances overall antimicrobial stewardship by balancing precision, ease, and resource efficiency.[[[49]](#ref-49)](#ref-49)

## Applications

### Clinical Microbiology

In clinical microbiology laboratories, broth microdilution serves as a reference method for determining antimicrobial susceptibility of key pathogens, such as [Enterobacteriaceae](/page/Enterobacteriaceae) and [Staphylococcus](/page/Staphylococcus) species, isolated from bloodstream infections. This technique quantifies the [minimum inhibitory concentration](/page/Minimum_inhibitory_concentration) (MIC) of antibiotics, enabling clinicians to transition from empirical therapy—often broad-spectrum agents—to targeted treatments that optimize efficacy while minimizing toxicity and resistance development. For instance, in cases of bacteremia caused by multidrug-resistant [Enterobacteriaceae](/page/Enterobacteriaceae), broth microdilution results guide the selection of agents like [carbapenems](/page/Carbapenem) or beta-lactam/[beta-lactamase](/page/Beta-lactamase) inhibitor combinations, directly informing de-escalation strategies in patient care.[[[52]](#ref-52)](#ref-52)[[[41]](#ref-41)](#ref-41) Broth microdilution contributes significantly to [antimicrobial](/page/Antimicrobial) [surveillance](/page/Surveillance) efforts, including the compilation of [hospital](/page/Hospital) antibiograms and participation in national programs like the CDC's tracking of carbapenem-resistant [Enterobacteriaceae](/page/Enterobacteriaceae) (CRE). By providing precise MIC data, it helps establish local resistance patterns, such as the prevalence of CRE in [hospital](/page/Hospital) settings, which informs [infection](/page/Infection) control measures and [public health](/page/Public_health) responses. This method's quantitative output is essential for antimicrobial stewardship programs, as it supports the monitoring of resistance trends and the promotion of judicious [antibiotic](/page/Antibiotic) use to curb the spread of resistant strains.[[[53]](#ref-53)](#ref-53)[[[54]](#ref-54)](#ref-54)[[[41]](#ref-41)](#ref-41) In routine clinical workflows, broth microdilution is frequently automated through systems like VITEK 2, which employs colorimetric detection in 64-microwell cards to deliver susceptibility results in 6-12 hours for many Gram-negative and [Gram-positive bacteria](/page/Gram-positive_bacteria), accelerating turnaround times compared to manual methods. These automated platforms integrate seamlessly with laboratory information systems and are often paired with molecular identification tools, such as MALDI-TOF [mass spectrometry](/page/Mass_spectrometry), to enable rapid [pathogen](/page/Pathogen) detection followed by susceptibility testing. This combination enhances diagnostic efficiency in high-volume settings, allowing for timely reporting that aligns with urgent clinical needs.[[[55]](#ref-55)](#ref-55)[[[41]](#ref-41)](#ref-41) The application of broth microdilution in clinical [microbiology](/page/Microbiology) has demonstrated a measurable impact on patient outcomes, particularly in intensive care units (ICUs), where studies indicate it supports antimicrobial stewardship by reducing inappropriate [antibiotic](/page/Antibiotic) prescribing through evidence-based susceptibility data. For example, integration of MIC-guided therapy has been associated with decreases in [broad-spectrum antibiotic](/page/Broad-spectrum_antibiotic) use in stewardship interventions, leading to shorter hospital stays and lower rates of adverse events like [Clostridioides difficile](/page/Clostridioides_difficile) infections. These benefits underscore its role in improving infection control and resource allocation in hospital environments.[[[41]](#ref-41)](#ref-41)[[[56]](#ref-56)](#ref-56)[[[57]](#ref-57)](#ref-57)

### Research and Development

Broth microdilution has become a cornerstone in high-throughput drug screening for novel [antibiotics](/page/Antibiotic), enabling the evaluation of compound libraries against diverse bacterial panels. This method facilitates the testing of thousands of potential antimicrobials in 96-well formats, often against over 100 strains to identify hits with broad-spectrum activity. For instance, [phenotypic screening](/page/Phenotypic_screening) of DNA-encoded libraries comprising 7,488 members has utilized broth microdilution to deconvolute antibacterial leads by determining minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) against Gram-negative pathogens.[[[58]](#ref-58)](#ref-58) Similarly, quantitative liquid culture assays based on microdilution principles have screened bacterial extracts for antibiotic production, achieving rapid identification of active metabolites through automated MIC readouts. These approaches support combinatorial library evaluations, where serial dilutions in [broth](/page/Broth) allow precise MIC determination for structure-activity relationship studies, accelerating the discovery of compounds targeting [multidrug-resistant bacteria](/page/Multidrug-resistant_bacteria). In studies of [antimicrobial resistance](/page/Antimicrobial_resistance) mechanisms, broth microdilution quantifies MIC shifts in genetically engineered mutants, providing direct evidence of [efflux pump](/page/Efflux_pump) contributions to reduced susceptibility. Overexpression of efflux pumps, such as TolC in [Enterobacterales](/page/Enterobacterales), has been shown to elevate MICs for substrates like [ciprofloxacin](/page/Ciprofloxacin) by up to 32-fold compared to wild-type strains, as measured via standard broth microdilution protocols.[[[59]](#ref-59)](#ref-59) This technique also correlates phenotypic MIC data with genomic alterations; for example, mutations in regulators like MexZ in [Pseudomonas aeruginosa](/page/Pseudomonas_aeruginosa) lead to MexXY pump overexpression, shifting MICs for aminoglycosides by 4- to 8-fold and facilitating whole-genome sequencing validations of resistance loci.[[[60]](#ref-60)](#ref-60) Such analyses have elucidated how efflux systems export antibiotics, informing efflux inhibitor development and resistance surveillance. Adaptations of broth microdilution extend its utility to complex infection models, including [biofilms](/page/Biofilm) and pharmacodynamic simulations. The Calgary Biofilm Device integrates with microdilution to assess minimum biofilm eradication concentrations (MBECs), where biofilms formed on pegs are exposed to serial [antibiotic](/page/Antibiotic) dilutions, revealing tolerance levels often 10- to 1,000-fold higher than planktonic MICs for pathogens like [Staphylococcus aureus](/page/Staphylococcus_aureus).[[[61]](#ref-61)](#ref-61) For intracellular pathogens, modified protocols incorporate host cell co-cultures to evaluate [antibiotic](/page/Antibiotic) penetration, though challenges in standardization persist. In pharmacodynamic modeling, post-MIC time-kill curves generated from microdilution setups track bacterial killing kinetics; for [marbofloxacin](/page/Marbofloxacin) against [Escherichia coli](/page/Escherichia_coli), these curves demonstrate concentration-dependent bactericidal effects at 4-8 times the MIC, informing dosing regimens for time- and concentration-dependent antibiotics.[[[62]](#ref-62)](#ref-62) Recent innovations leverage broth microdilution data in [artificial intelligence](/page/Artificial_intelligence) applications to predict resistance patterns. Machine learning models trained on MIC datasets from microdilution assays across thousands of isolates forecast susceptibility for pathogens like [Salmonella](/page/Salmonella), achieving up to 95% accuracy by integrating genomic features with phenotypic profiles.[[[63]](#ref-63)](#ref-63) This integration aids in rapid resistance [surveillance](/page/Surveillance) without exhaustive culturing. Furthermore, broth microdilution plays a pivotal role in research on WHO priority pathogens, such as carbapenem-resistant [Enterobacteriaceae](/page/Enterobacteriaceae), where standardized MIC testing evaluates novel agents like [cefiderocol](/page/Cefiderocol) against critical threats, guiding global drug development pipelines.

Standard Document

Twelfth Edition

Microbiology

### CLSI M07

#### Methods for Dilution Antimicrobial Susceptibility Tests for Bacteria That Grow Aerobically

CLSI M07 provides essential guidance for antimicrobial susceptibility testing of aerobic bacteria using broth macrodilution, broth microdilution, and agar dilution methods. This standard is crucial for determining the susceptibility of organisms involved in infectious processes when resistance patterns cannot be reliably predicted based on species identity.

Designed for laboratories performing susceptibility testing, M07 ensures accurate and standardized minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) determinations. Every purchase includes the M07-Ed12 Quick Guide for streamlined reference. Discounted package pricing is available when purchasing the latest editions of CLSI M02, M07, and M100 together.

March 19, 2024

Barbara L. Zimmer, PhD

Abstract

Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute M07—Methods for Dilution Antimicrobial Susceptibility Tests for Bacteria That Grow Aerobically offers guidance for antimicrobial susceptibility testing that is indicated for any organism that contributes to an infectious process warranting antimicrobial chemotherapy, if its susceptibility cannot be reliably predicted from knowledge of the organism’s identity. Susceptibility tests are most often indicated when the causative organism is thought to belong to a species capable of exhibiting resistance to commonly used antimicrobial agents. Various laboratory methods can be used to measure the in vitro susceptibility of bacteria to antimicrobial agents. Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute M07—Methods for Dilution Antimicrobial Susceptibility Tests for Bacteria That Grow Aerobically describes standard broth dilution (macrodilution and microdilution [the microdilution method described in CLSI M07 is the same methodology outlined in ISO 20776-1])1 and agar dilution techniques, and it includes a series of procedures to standardize the way the tests are performed. The performance, applications, and limitations of the current CLSI-recommended methods are also described. The supplemental information (CLSI M1002 tables) used with this standard represents the most current information for drug selection, interpretation, and QC using the procedures standardized in CLSI M07.

Overview of Changes

This standard replaces CLSI M07-Ed11, published in 2018, with several updates. General changes include revised information for testing and reporting, updated nomenclature from “coagulase-negative staphylococci” (CoNS) to “staphylococci other than Staphylococcus aureus” (SOSA), and changes in terminology from “groups” to “tiers” and “microdilution trays” to “microdilution panels.” Definitions and abbreviations have been updated, with new definitions added and some deleted. Subchapters on selecting antimicrobial agents, equivalent agents, and suggested guidelines for testing and reporting have been clarified and updated, including the addition of new tables for antimicrobial agent tiers and designations. New information on preparing and storing diluted antimicrobial agents, inoculum preparation, and inoculation methods has been included, along with updates to agar dilution procedures and special considerations for fastidious organisms and resistance detection.

Additional changes include the addition of iron-depleted cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth for testing cefiderocol and Mueller-Hinton fastidious broth for fastidious organisms. The document now includes step-action tables for colony counts and new subchapters on interpreting results and determining end points. Special considerations for detecting resistance have been revised, with new information on mecC isolates, vancomycin-resistant and vancomycin-intermediate S. aureus, AmpC enzymes, and carbapenemases. The standard also provides recommendations for selecting quality control strains and updates to conditions for dilution antimicrobial susceptibility tests, including new acceptable media for nonfastidious and fastidious organisms.

Scope

This standard describes standard broth (macrodilution and microdilution) and agar dilution methods for determining in vitro susceptibility to antimicrobial agents for bacteria that grow aerobically and includes:

• Broth and agar dilution test preparation

• Testing conditions, including inoculum preparation and standardization, incubation time, and incubation temperature

• Results interpretation

• QC procedures

• Dilution test method limitations

To assist the medical laboratory, recommendations are provided for selecting antimicrobial agents for routine testing and reporting. Standards for testing the in vitro antimicrobial susceptibility of bacteria that grow aerobically using the antimicrobial disk testing method are found in CLSI M02. Standards for testing the in vitro antimicrobial susceptibility of bacteria that grow anaerobically are found in CLSI M11. Guidelines for standardized antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) of infrequently isolated or fastidious bacteria that are not included in CLSI M02, M07, or M11 are available in CLSI M45.

The AST methods provided in this standard can be used in laboratories around the world, including but not limited to:

• Medical laboratories

• Public health laboratories

• Research laboratories

• Food laboratories

• Environmental laboratories

# Development of a Broth Microdilution Method for Exebacase Susceptibility Testing

### Jun T Oh

### Jane E Ambler

### Cara Cassino

### Raymond Schuch

**Citation** Oh JT, Ambler JE, Cassino C, Schuch R. 2021. Development of a broth microdilution method for exebacase susceptibility testing. Antimicrob Agents Chemother 65:e02587-20. <https://doi.org/10.1128/AAC.02587-20>.

Corresponding author.

Received 2020 Dec 10; Revision requested 2021 Jan 10; Accepted 2021 Apr 14; Prepublished 2021 Apr 26; Collection date 2021 Jul.

This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the [Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).

## ABSTRACT

Exebacase (CF-301) belongs to a new class of protein-based antibacterial agents, known as lysins (peptidoglycan hydrolases). Exebacase, a novel lysin with antistaphylococcal activity, is in phase 3 of clinical development. To advance into the clinic, it was necessary to develop an accurate and reproducible method for exebacase MIC determination. The Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) reference broth microdilution (BMD) method using cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth (CAMHB) produced trailing MIC endpoints, and exebacase activity was diminished when frozen BMD panels were used. A modified BMD method was developed using CAMHB supplemented with 25% horse serum and 0.5 mM dl-dithiothreitol (CAMHB-HSD). Preliminary quality control (QC) ranges for *Staphylococcus aureus* ATCC 29213 of 0.25 to 1 μg/ml and for *Enterococcus faecalis* ATCC 29212 of 16 to 64 μg/ml were determined based on the results of a CLSI M23-defined MIC QC tier 1 study. These preliminary QC ranges validated the MIC data generated from a systematic study testing a discrete *S. aureus* strain collection using CAMHB-HSD to investigate the impact of parameters known to influence susceptibility test results and to evaluate the exebacase MIC distribution against clinical *S. aureus* isolates. Presentation of these data led to the CLSI Subcommittee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing (AST) approval of the use of CAMHB-HSD to determine exebacase susceptibility and commencement of a multilaboratory (tier 2) QC study. Use of a standard BMD method and concomitant QC testing provides confidence in the assessment of test performance to generate accurate and reproducible susceptibility data during antibacterial drug development.

**KEYWORDS:** direct lytic agents, lysin, endolysin, exebacase, MIC, CLSI, CF-301

## INTRODUCTION

A promising new therapeutic approach to killing antibiotic-resistant bacterial pathogens is based on the use of direct lytic agents (DLAs), including lysins, which represent a new antimicrobial modality currently in clinical development ([1](#B1)). Lysins are recombinantly produced cell wall peptidoglycan hydrolytic enzymes that elicit rapid cleavage of the cell wall and resultant osmotic lysis ([1](#B1), [2](#B2)). Exebacase is an antistaphylococcal lysin with the following microbiological attributes: (i) rapid, targeted bactericidal activity, (ii) the ability to eradicate staphylococcal biofilms, (iii) synergy with antistaphylococcal antibiotics, (iv) a low propensity for the development of resistance, (v) no cross-resistance with antibiotics, and (v) an extended *in vitro* and *in vivo* postantibiotic effect ([3](#B3)[–](#B4)[7](#B7)).

Antibacterial susceptibility testing is the mainstay of antibacterial drug development, providing important data to assess the potential clinical efficacy of an investigational drug for the intended indication(s). While for many traditional antibiotics the CLSI reference broth microdilution (BMD) method is used to assess susceptibility, it is recognized that for some drugs, in particular those targeting difficult-to-treat multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria, modification of the reference method is necessary ([8](#B8)[–](#B9)[10](#B10)). Such modifications include supplementation of cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth (CAMHB) with Ca2+ (for daptomycin [[11](#B11), [12](#B12)]) or polysorbate (Tween) 80 (for dalbavancin and oritavancin [[12](#B12), [13](#B13)]) and the use of iron-depleted CAMHB prepared with Chelex 100 resin (for cefiderocol [[12](#B12), [14](#B14)]). Tigecycline and omadacycline susceptibility testing requires the use of freshly prepared medium (≤12 h), noting that panels may be frozen for later use ([14](#B14), [15](#B15)). Given the recent rise in drugs that necessitate a modified BMD method, it is likely that drugs requiring medium modifications will become more commonplace, in particular with future investigational nontraditional antibacterial agents having novel clinical development programs to address unmet medical needs with regard to specific MDR pathogens.

A standardized MIC assay for a therapeutic lysin has not previously been described, nor has a protein-based direct lytic agent (e.g., a nonantibiotic) been evaluated by a recognized standards development organization, such as CLSI. Before conducting clinical trials in the United States, drug sponsors must describe the methods used for generating susceptibility data by referencing a recognized standards development organization, such as CLSI, or evaluate susceptibility by other methods, including modification of the method. Herein, exebacase MIC data generated using the CLSI reference BMD method are presented and compared with analogous data generated using CAMHB supplemented with 25% horse serum and 0.5 mM dl-dithiothreitol (CAMHB-HSD) against a discrete collection of *Staphylococcus aureus* isolates. Preliminary MIC quality control (QC) ranges for *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *Enterococcus faecalis* ATCC 29212 were then identified in accordance with CLSI M23-A4 using the proposed modified method. The preliminary QC ranges served to validate the exebacase MIC distribution generated by testing 149 *S. aureus* clinical isolates as well as the MIC results of an M23-defined systematic study conducted with CAMHB-HSD to investigate the impact of parameters known to influence susceptibility test results. Based on these studies, the CLSI Subcommittee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing (AST) approved the use of CAMHB-HSD to determine exebacase MICs. A multilaboratory MIC (tier 2) QC study was subsequently initiated to establish expected QC ranges for the new BMD method ([30](#B30)). The BMD method for the determination of exebacase susceptibility and the expected QC ranges for exebacase, which based on the tier 2 study results were defined as 0.25 to 2 μg/ml for *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and 8 to 64 μg/ml for *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212, have been published in CLSI document M100 ([12](#B12)).

## RESULTS

### Limitations of the CLSI reference BMD method for exebacase MIC determination.

Exebacase MICs were determined in triplicate using CAMHB alone against a discrete heterogenous collection of 25 *S. aureus* (methicillin-susceptible *S. aureus* [MSSA] and methicillin-resistant *S. aureus* [MRSA]) isolates, including various clinical and laboratory-derived resistant phenotypes (see Table S1 in the supplemental material) in accordance with CLSI M07-A11 recommendations ([9](#B9)). Exebacase MICs generated with CAMHB alone ranged from 2 to 128 μg/ml, and MIC50 and MIC90 values were 32 and 64 μg/ml ([Table 1](#T1)). MIC endpoints were obscured by trailing for all of the isolates tested, including the QC strains, as illustrated by the community-acquired methicillin-resistant *S. aureus* strain NRS 123 (USA 400) ([Fig. 1](#F1)). Marked trailing was evident over a range of exebacase concentrations at and above the MIC endpoint of 64 μg/ml.

#### TABLE 1.

#### FIG 1.

![FIG 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/2b91/8218677/fdd9a098d39f/aac.02587-20-f001.jpg)

![FIG 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/2b91/8218677/fdd9a098d39f/aac.02587-20-f001.jpg)

Moreover, exebacase activity was diminished when frozen BMD MIC panels were used. The impact of freeze-thaw conditions was assessed by replicating conditions associated with the preparation and use of frozen BMD panels in-house or by a commercial manufacturer to support the susceptibility testing of clinical trial isolates by a central microbiology laboratory used in clinical trials ([15](#B15)). Using thawed exebacase BMD panels, originally prepared and frozen in CAMHB at −80°C until use, MICs ranged from 32 to >256 μg/ml and MIC50 and MIC90 values were 128 and 256 μg/ml against the same collection of 25 clinical *S. aureus* isolates ([Table 2](#T2)). The reduction in exebacase activity seen with thawed BMD panels compared to freshly prepared BMD panels was marked (approximately 2- to 16-fold).

#### TABLE 2.

Impact of a freeze-thaw cycle on exebacase MICs against 25 *S. aureus* isolates

| Medium[*a*](#T2F1) | Freeze-thaw | MIC (μg/ml)  --- | | | Trailing |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 50% | 90% | Range | effect |
| CAMHB | No | 32 | 64 | 2–128 | Yes |
|  | Yes | 128 | 256 | 32–>256 | Yes |
| CAMHB, 25% horse serum | No | 0.5 | 2 | 0.125–2 | No |
|  | Yes | 2 | 2 | 0.5–8 | No |
| CAMHB, 0.5 mM DTT | No | 128 | 128 | 8–256 | Yes |
|  | Yes | 128 | 256 | 64–>256 | Yes |
| CAMHB, 25% horse serum, 0.5 mM DTT | No | 0.5 | 1 | 0.25–2 | No |
|  | Yes | 0.5 | 1 | 0.25–2 | No |

Medium was prepared using reagents from the following sources: CAMHB (BBL Mueller-Hinton broth II, cation adjusted), Becton Dickinson, catalog number 212322; horse serum (donor herd), Sigma-Aldrich, catalog number H1270; and DTT, Sigma-Aldrich, catalog number 646563.

### Standard CLSI M23-A4 supplements do not reduce trailing.

To eliminate or reduce trailing and thereby improve accurate MIC determination, MIC testing was conducted using CAMHB supplemented with either polysorbate 80, Ca2+, NaCl, or laked horse blood as described elsewhere ([8](#B8), [16](#B16)). None of the standard supplements tested diminished trailing ([Table 1](#T1) and data not shown). In addition, testing was performed using polypropylene plates with and without bovine serum albumin in place of polystyrene plates (as described for cationic antimicrobial peptides to reduce adherence to plastic surfaces [[17](#B17), [18](#B18)]); however, their use did not reduce trailing. Supplementation of CAMHB with either bovine serum albumin alone or with NaCl (with and without agitation at 200 rpm), as described for lysostaphin MIC determination ([19](#B19)), also did not impact trailing, nor did the incubation of assay plates in a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 35°C ± 2°C or supplementation of CAMHB with either 25 mM or 44 mM NaHCO3, as described by Ersoy et al. ([20](#B20)).

### Supplementation with horse serum eliminates trailing.

We previously reported a marked (∼64-fold) reduction in exebacase MICs observed in the presence of human serum compared to MICs generated in CAMHB alone ([28](#B28)). Based on these findings and taken with the precedent of using horse blood in AST of *Streptococcus* spp. ([12](#B12)), the impact of horse serum on exebacase AST was examined against the collection of 25 *S. aureus* isolates. The use of horse serum resulted in an exebacase MIC range of 0.125 to 2 μg/ml (MIC50 and MIC90 values of 0.5 and 2 μg/ml) and eliminated the trailing when diluted in CAMHB to concentrations of at least 25% ([Table 1](#T1)). Horse serum concentrations at or below 12.5% resulted in MIC increases greater than 2 log2 dilutions compared to values obtained with higher concentrations of horse serum.

### dl-Dithiothreitol stabilizes exebacase activity in CAMHB with 25% horse serum.

The use of CAMHB supplemented with 25% horse serum remained hampered by a loss of activity when frozen exebacase BMD panels were used, compared to freshly prepared BMD panels. The thawed BMD panels yielded MIC50 and MIC90 values of 2 and 2 μg/ml (MIC range of 0.5 to 8 μg/ml) for the 25 *S. aureus* isolates, compared to MIC50 and MIC90 values of 0.5 and 2 μg/ml (MIC range of 0.125 to 2 μg/ml) obtained with freshly prepared BMD panels ([Table 2](#T2)).

To stabilize exebacase activity after thawing, we considered the enzymatic nature of exebacase and the need to protect its active-site cysteine residue from air oxidation and inactivation using the reducing agent dl-dithiothreitol (DTT). Notably, DTT protects the catalytic site cysteines of thiol enzymes, like exebacase, by preventing the oxidation of sulfhydryl groups ([21](#B21)). When exebacase activity against *S. aureus* strain NRS 123 was determined in CAMHB supplemented with both 25% horse serum and a range of DTT concentrations (0.01 to 2 mM), the activity was stabilized (i.e., there was no difference in observed MICs obtained using freshly prepared CAMHB-HSD medium versus previously frozen CAMHB-HSD medium) at DTT concentrations of ≥0.5 mM (Table S2). When the collection of 25 *S. aureus* isolates was tested using CAMHB supplemented with 25% horse serum and 0.5 mM DTT (CAMHB-HSD) and results were compared to MIC data generated using freshly prepared BMD panels, no differences in exebacase MIC ranges or MIC50 and MIC90 values were observed ([Table 2](#T2)). An example of clear exebacase MIC endpoints in CAMHB-HSD against strain NRS 123 are shown in [Fig. 2](#F2). Supplementation with DTT alone (i.e., without 25% horse serum), however, resulted in much higher MIC50 and MIC90 values for both freshly prepared and frozen/thawed BMD panels (128 and 128 μg/ml and 128 and 256 μg/ml, respectively), and, for this reason, supplementation with both 25% horse serum and 0.5 mM DTT is required.

#### FIG 2.

![FIG 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/2b91/8218677/029bc4100ffc/aac.02587-20-f002.jpg)

![FIG 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/2b91/8218677/029bc4100ffc/aac.02587-20-f002.jpg)

MIC determination in triplicate against *S. aureus* strain NRS 123 in CAMHB-HSD. A clear and unambiguous MIC is observed at 0.5 μg/ml. GC, growth control; SC, sterility control.

### Growth rates in CAMHB-HSD and CAMHB are similar.

The growth kinetics of the CLSI reference QC strain *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 in CAMHB-HSD and in CAMHB alone were compared. Over a 24-h period, aerated cultures grown in CAMHB-HSD and in CAMHB alone demonstrated equivalent growth kinetics (Fig. S1). Furthermore, the doubling times in CAMHB-HSD and CAMHB were similar, at 39 and 41 min, respectively.

### Low MIC variability associated with different commercial sources of CAMHB and DTT.

The use of two different commercial sources of dehydrated CAMHB and preprepared CAMHB generated similar MIC data: MIC50 and MIC90 of 0.5 and 1 μg/ml (MIC range of 0.25 to 2 μg/ml) and MIC50 and MIC90 of 1 and 1 μg/ml (MIC range of 0.5 to 2 μg/ml), respectively (Table S3). Different commercial sources of DTT were likewise found to have little impact on MIC variability, with MIC50 and MIC90 (and range) values for both freshly prepared and frozen/thawed panels of 0.5 and 1 μg/ml (0.25 to 2 μg/ml) (Table S4).

### Low MIC variability associated with different testing conditions using CAMHB-HSD.

The impact of variations in medium pH, bacterial inoculum, and incubation duration and conditions (temperature and CO2) were investigated using the 25 clinical *S. aureus* isolates. Standard testing conditions in CAMHB-HSD relied on a medium pH value of 7.4, a bacterial inoculum of 5 × 105 CFU/ml, and incubation at 35°C ± 2°C in ambient air for 18 h; these conditions resulted in MIC50 and MIC90 values of 0.5 and 1 μg/ml (MIC range of 0.25 to 2 μg/ml) (Table S5). Variations in medium pH (including pH values of 7.0 and 8.0), bacterial inoculum (5 × 104, 1 × 105, and 5 × 106), incubation duration (24 and 48 h), and incubation conditions (ambient air at 30°C and in a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 35°C ± 2°C) yielded very similar MIC data with differences of <2 log2 dilutions. Exebacase MIC determination in CAMHB-HSD is not overtly impacted by minor methodological variations.

### Twelve-month stability of exebacase in frozen BMD panels.

Freshly prepared exebacase BMD panels were frozen promptly at −80°C and thawed at multiple intervals for up to 1 year, prior to the determination of MIC endpoints against the 25 *S. aureus* isolates. The exebacase MIC range and MIC50 and MIC90 generated from frozen and thawed BMD panels were stable for at least 12 months, with values of either 0.5 and 0.5 μg/ml (0.25 to 1 μg/ml) or 0.5 and 1 μg/ml (0.25 to 2 μg/ml) (Table S6). No trending was observed with the MIC data generated during the 12-month study period.

### CLSI M23-defined preliminary QC study.

A CLSI M23 preliminary MIC QC tier 1 study was conducted using CAMHB-HSD ([8](#B8)) at two independent testing laboratories (The Clinical Microbiology Institute, Wilsonville, OR, and the ContraFect Research Laboratory, Yonkers, NY) to establish preliminary exebacase MIC QC ranges for appropriate reference QC strains, *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212. The MIC results generated by both laboratories for *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212 using two different CAMHB lots from the same commercial manufacturer are presented in [Table 3](#T3). A total of 50 MIC results were generated by each testing laboratory (25 replicate results for each ATCC strain) over 6 days using two different lots of CAMHB broth supplemented with 25% horse serum and 0.5 mM dl-dithiothreitol. For *S. aureus* ATCC 29213, the overall distribution of MIC results was 2 log2 dilution steps (0.5 to 1 μg/ml), 98.0% of results at the mode (0.5 μg/ml) and 100% of results within ±1 doubling dilution of the mode. For *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212, the overall distribution of MIC results was 2 log2 dilution steps (16 to 32 μg/ml), 86.0% of results at the mode (32 μg/ml), and 100% within ±1 doubling dilution of the mode. Preliminary QC ranges of 0.25 to 1 μg/ml for *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and 16 to 64 μg/ml for *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212 were identified.

### Exebacase *in vitro* activity against 149 clinical *S. aureus* isolates.

The *in vitro* activity of exebacase was determined against a previously described collection of clinical *S. aureus* (74 MSSA and 75 MRSA) isolates using frozen BMD CAMHB-HSD panels. The exebacase MIC distribution ranged from 0.12 to 1 μg/ml, the modal MIC of 0.5 μg/ml represented 83% of the isolates tested, and MIC50 and MIC90 values were 0.5 and 0.5 μg/ml and 0.5 and 1 μg/ml for MSSA and MRSA isolates, respectively ([Table 4](#T4)) and are consistent with data we reported previously for these isolates tested in undiluted human serum ([28](#B28)).

### Investigation of MIC variability using different commercial sources of horse serum.

At the request of the CLSI Subcommittee on AST, a CLSI-style tier 1 QC study was conducted to evaluate various commercial sources of horse serum on the test performance of the CAMHB-HSD medium in light of significant interlaboratory variability associated with antibiotic susceptibility testing of pneumococci ([22](#B22), [23](#B23)). Multiple horse serum samples were tested (see Table S7). Five replicates of each QC strain were tested each day, for 5 separate days, i.e., a total of 25 replicates of each serum sample, generating a total of 350 exebacase MIC results for each QC organism. For *S. aureus* ATCC 29213, 98.6% of MICs were within ±1 doubling dilution of the mode (0.5 μg/ml), and for *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212, 100% were within ±1 doubling dilution of the mode (32 μg/ml) ([Table 5](#T5)). Importantly, CAMHB-HSD medium prepared with each of the 14 horse serum samples produced MIC ranges within the exebacase QC ranges established in a multilaboratory QC tier 1 and subsequent tier 2 study ([12](#B12)).

## DISCUSSION

Lysins are a novel class of antibacterial agents, distinct from conventional antibiotics with respect to chemical/physical characteristics and an enzymatic mechanism of action based on rapid cell wall hydrolysis and osmotic lysis. Consideration of these differences gave rise to the unique modifications tested in our studies to establish a new reference BMD method for accurate and reproducible MIC determination. The use of horse serum at a concentration of 25% specifically abrogated the distinctive trailing effect observed when CAMHB alone was used and yielded MIC data equivalent to data generated using human serum alone. Supplementation with horse serum enabled a better understanding of antistaphylococcal activity in a biologically relevant environment, compared to MIC data generated using CAMHB alone.

While the inclusion of 25% horse serum in CAMHB-HSD mitigated the observed trailing MIC endpoints and thereby facilitated the reading of exebacase MIC endpoints, the inclusion of 0.5 mM DTT served to stabilize exebacase and thereby facilitate the long-term storage (up to 12 months) of frozen BMD panels. The mechanism of stabilization is understood in light of the enzymatic nature of exebacase, which is a cysteine/histidine-dependent amidohydrolase/peptidase with an active-site cysteine residue that is subject to air oxidation ([3](#B3), [24](#B24)). A strong reducing agent such as DTT likely prevents oxidation of sulfhydryl groups in cysteine that may occur during the handling of frozen exebacase BMD panels. DTT is a critical supplement to facilitate large-scale MIC panel preparation by a central laboratory for testing clinical isolates from antibacterial surveillance programs and clinical trials.

As the first AST medium to be described for MIC determination of a lysin enzyme, CAMHB-HSD allows accurate and reproducible MIC determination, and concomitant QC testing allows evaluation of test performance. The medium provided consistent, reproducible MIC results across a range of clinical *S. aureus* isolates tested, including various resistant phenotypes, and across a variety of testing conditions. Variations in the commercial source of individual medium components, including CAMHB, DTT, and horse serum, had no impact on MICs determined using either freshly prepared or frozen BMD panels. There was, likewise, no impact on MICs associated with variations tested in medium pH, culture inoculum, incubation temperature and atmosphere, or incubation length. Importantly, MICs obtained using BMD panels stored for up to 12 months at −80°C exhibited minor MIC variation, of ≤1 log2 dilution.

The BMD method described herein and expected exebacase QC ranges identified from a subsequent multilaboratory CLSI M23-defined MIC tier 2 study (0.25 to 2 μg/ml for *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and 8 to 64 μg/ml for *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212) are now published in CLSI M100 ([12](#B12)). The BMD method and concomitant QC testing were used to evaluate exebacase susceptibility against clinical *S. aureus* isolates in a recent surveillance study ([25](#B25)) and in the completed phase 2 clinical trial ([NCT03163446](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03163446)) ([26](#B26)). The test method will furthermore be used to support antibacterial surveillance studies and the ongoing superiority designed phase 3 ([NCT04160468](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04160468)) DISRUPT study to evaluate the efficacy and safety of exebacase in addition to antistaphylococcal antibiotics compared with antibiotics alone for the treatment of *S. aureus* bloodstream infections, including right-sided infective endocarditis ([27](#B27)).

## MATERIALS AND METHODS

### Bacterial strains.

The discrete collection of 25 *S. aureus* strains tested in CAMHB and subsequently used throughout the study to identify a suitable medium for testing exebacase susceptibility and assess the influence of various parameters known to impact MIC results is presented in Table S1. The collection of 149 clinical *S. aureus* isolates (74 MSSA and 75 MRSA) used to validate the test performance of the exebacase AST medium was obtained from JMI Laboratories (North Liberty, IA) and was described previously ([5](#B5), [28](#B28)). *Staphylococcus aureus* ATCC 29213 and *Enterococcus faecalis* ATCC 29212 were selected as reference QC strains for M23-style tier 1 studies ([8](#B8)).

### Materials and chemicals.

Exebacase was obtained from ContraFect Corporation (lot number NBA0467-08). Growth media (and sources) are as follows: BBL Mueller-Hinton broth II, cation adjusted (Becton, Dickinson and company [BD]; catalog number 296164, lot number 6054984); BBL Mueller-Hinton II broth, cation adjusted (BD; catalog number 212322, lot numbers 2089488 and 5257869); BBL Mueller-Hinton II broth, cation adjusted (BD; catalog number 297963, lot numbers 6014547 and 4044343); BBL Mueller-Hinton broth II (Sigma-Aldrich; catalog number 90922, lot number BCBR3303V); and BBL Mueller-Hinton II broth, cation adjusted (Teknova; catalog number M5860, lot number M586012B1601). Horse serum was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (catalog number H1270, lot number 15G382), BioreclamationIVT (catalog number HSESRM, lot number HSE1225), Gibco (catalog number 16050-122, lot number 1671315), and Corning (catalog number 35-030-CV, lot number 35030105). Laked horse blood was obtained from Hema Resource and Supply, Inc. (catalog number 15-14-0050-28). Additional sources of horse serum are described in Table S7. Bovine serum albumin (catalog number A2153, lot number SLBL5462V), dl-dithiothreitol solution (catalog number 646563, lot numbers MKBW5575V and MKBX878V), dl-dithiothreitol (catalog number 43815-25G, lot number BCBBD7009V), and Tween 80 (catalog number P4780, lot number MKBW2896V) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. dl-Dithiothreitol was also obtained from G-Biosciences (catalog number RC-046, lot number 151106). Sodium chloride (catalog number BP358-10, lot number 150661) and calcium chloride (catalog number C77-212, lot number 110651) were obtained from Fisher Scientific.

### MIC determination by broth microdilution.

Assays were performed according to CLSI M07-A11 guidelines ([9](#B9)) in triplicate, with the exception of the indicated procedural variations and use of nonstandard AST media. The experimental parameters were varied one at a time during testing, and results were compared with those obtained using the standard reference BMD method (CAMHB). Falcon 96-well polystyrene plates (Corning; catalog number 351177) were used in all assays, except for indicated analyses using Falcon 96-well polypropylene plates (Corning; catalog number 351190). Assay plates were incubated for 18 h at 35 ± 2°C in ambient air, and MICs were determined visually unless otherwise indicated. To investigate the impact of freeze-thawing on exebacase activity in dilution panels (in indicated media), plates were transferred to a −80°C freezer within 2 h of preparation; after 24 h (unless otherwise indicated), the plates were removed and thawed for 1 h in ambient air prior to the addition of inocula (final cell density = 5 × 105 CFU/ml) and overnight incubation at 35°C ± 2°C to determine MICs.

### Preparation of CAMHB-HSD.

The CAMHB was prepared and stored according to each of the manufacturer’s recommendations. Horse serum was stored frozen at −20°C and thawed in a 24°C water bath for 30 min prior to use. After thawing, the horse serum was aseptically added to CAMHB to a final concentration of 25% and swirled to mix. The dl-dithiothreitol was then added aseptically to a final concentration of 0.5 mM and swirled to mix. Broth pH was typically between 7.2 and 7.4 after the supplementation. The CAMHB-HSD medium was used within 2 h of preparation.

### Growth kinetics in CAMHB-HSD.

The growth of *S. aureus* strain ATCC 29213 was examined in both the reference BMD medium (CAMHB) and CAMHB-HSD. An overnight culture was diluted by a factor of 1:100 into each medium and incubated at 35°C ± 2°C with agitation at 150 rpm for 24 h. Culture samples were collected every hour for 2 h and then every 30 min for the next 6 h (with a final collection at 24 h). For each time point, optical density (OD) at 600 nm was recorded. The optical densities were plotted on a log10 scale. Each condition was examined in triplicate, and mean values with standard deviation are shown. Culture doubling times were calculated exactly as described elsewhere ([29](#B29)).

### CLSI M23-A5 tier 1 study.

Testing was performed at the Clinical Microbiology Institute (Wilsonville, OR) and at ContraFect (Yonkers, NY). Each lab used a unique lot of CAMHB II from Becton, Dickinson and Company (Clinical Microbiology Institute, lot number 4044343; ContraFect, lot number 20894988). The broths were autoclaved, cooled, and then supplemented with 25% horse serum (Sigma-Aldrich, lot number 15G382) plus 0.5 mM DTT (dl-dithiothreitol solution) (Sigma-Aldrich; lot number MKBW8225V) by aseptic addition. Broth pH was between 7.2 and 7.4 after additions at both laboratories. Working solution and test dilutions of exebacase were prepared in CAMHB-HSD. Vancomycin (Clinical Microbiology Institute, Sigma-Aldrich lot number SLBM3283V; ContraFect, Sigma-Aldrich lot number 120M14595V) and oxacillin (Clinical Microbiology Institute only; Sigma-Aldrich lot number 035M4889V) were tested in CAMHB or CAMHB plus 2% saline. Twenty-five replicates of two QC strains, *S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29212, were tested over 6 separate days. All inocula were prepared in 0.85% saline to match a McFarland 0.5 turbidity standard using colonies from a plate that has been incubated for 18 to 24 h. Each replicate was tested using a unique 0.5 McFarland preparation. Each inoculum preparation was further diluted with saline to obtain a final inoculum of approximately 5 × 105 CFU/ml in the test panel. Colony counts were performed daily.

### Tier 1 CLSI QC study to investigate the test performance using horse serum from different commercial sources.

An M23-A5 tier 1 QC study was performed at the Clinical Microbiology Institute (Wilsonville, OR) using CAMHB-HSD prepared with each of 14 different sources (Table S7) of non-heat-inactivated horse serum. Single lots of both CAMHB (Becton, Dickinson and Company; lot number 5257869) and DTT (Sigma-Aldrich; lot number MKBX878V) were used. Serial 2-fold dilutions of exebacase were prepared in each of the 14 lots of CAMHB-HSD. All dilutions were dispensed into 96-well polystyrene plates and frozen at −80°C until the day of use. Vancomycin powder purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (lot number SLBM3283V) was tested using CAMHB alone. For each lot of CAMHB-HSD, 5 to 8 replicates of each of the two QC strains (*S. aureus* ATCC 29213 and *E. faecalis* ATCC 29213) were tested each day for 5 separate days for a total of 25 replicates. Separate McFarland inocula were used for each test replicate each day. Inocula were prepared in 0.85% saline and adjusted to match a McFarland 0.5 turbidity standard using colonies from blood agar plates that had been incubated at 35°C ± 2°C for 18 to 24 h. Each replicate was tested using a unique inoculum preparation. Each inoculum preparation was further diluted to obtain a final inoculum of approximate 5 × 105 CFU/ml in the MIC test panel. Colony counts were performed on 1 replicate of each strain per day.
